head.ce:title: XG and CS-based self-assembled nanocomposite hydrogel embedding fluorescent NCQDs capable of detection and adsorptive removal of the polar MO and Cr(VI) pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Chemistry Research Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences, Shahid Beheshti University, P.O. Box: 1983969411, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Polymer Chemistry Research Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences, Shahid Beheshti University, P. O. Box: 1983969411, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aiming at dealing with organic and inorganic pollutants dissolved in aquatic environments, we introduce self-assembled fluorescent nanocomposite hydrogel based on a binary polysaccharide network (xanthan gum/chitosan) embedding nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots not only as a hybrid solid optical sensor for detecting Cr(VI) ions but also to remove anionically charged contaminants Cr(VI) and methyl orange (MO) by acting as an adsorbent. This fluorescent nanocomposite achieved a detection limit of 0.29 μM when used to detect Cr(VI) and demonstrated a fluorescence quantum yield of 59.7 %. Several factors contributed to the effectiveness of the adsorption of Cr(VI) and MO in batch studies, including the solution pH, dosage of the adsorbent, temperature, initial contamination level, and contact time. Experimental results showed 456 mg/g maximum adsorption capacity at pH 4 for MO compared to 291 mg/g at pH 2 for Cr(VI) at 25 °C. In addition to conforming to Langmuir's model, Cr(VI) and MO's adsorption kinetics closely matched pseudo-second-order. Using thermodynamic parameters, the results indicate that Cr(VI) and MO adsorb spontaneously and exothermically. Recycling spent adsorbent for Cr(VI) and MO using NaOH at 0.1 M was possible; the respective adsorption efficiency remained at approximately 82.2 % and 83 % after the fifth regeneration cycle.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: There is no doubt that we are facing one of the world's most pressing and challenging issues today because of pollution in our water supplies. Pollution to a considerable degree in water comes from industrial wastewater that contains hazardous heavy metal ions and synthetic dyes. Since chromium (VI) and methyl orange (MO) are non-degradable and carcinogenic, organic and inorganic water pollutants present grave health risks to humans and aquatic organisms (Bolisetty et al., 2019). The targeted contaminant, MO (Fig. S1) is a prevalent anionic azo dye commonly utilized in the manufacture of paper manufacturing, textiles, foodstuffs, and pharmaceuticals, and it is notoriously harmful to human health (Chena et al., 2018; Elgamal et al., 2023). Wastewater pollution triggered by inorganic pollutants, particularly chromium metal has turned into a serious concern along with dye pollution. Having the ability to dissolve extremely well in water, the chromium ions (Cr(VI)) are prevalent pollutants abounding on aquatic ecosystems that harm aquatic life (Sharma et al., 2022; Zheng et al., 2019). They are commonly generated during industrial operations including paint production, electroplating, leather tanning, textiles, and other suppliers of chemicals (Baral et al., 2006; Qi et al., 2016). Due to the risk of serious diseases caused by long-term exposure to Cr(VI) ions, it is strongly advised not to drink water containing >0.05 mg/L of Cr(VI) ions in accordance with the World Health Organization (WHO) (Chena et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2017). It is therefore a grave challenge for today's generation to minimize these pollutants and attain their optimal concentrations at the water source before releasing them into the environment. The focus of wastewater treatment research has largely been on developing materials that are affordable and capable of detecting Cr(VI) and removing Cr(VI) and MO pollutants.

For removing polar organic and inorganic pollutants from water, several techniques have been used previously. These are ion exchange, precipitation by chemical means, membrane filtration, coagulation, bio-adsorption, adsorption, and so on (Obotey Ezugbe & Rathilal, 2020; Huang et al., 2019; Iwuozor, 2019). Regarding each purification method's benefits and drawbacks, adsorption is still regarded due to its effectiveness, its easy operation, economy, and environmental friendliness because low levels of waste produced and reusability of the exhausted adsorbent as a viable method for removing Cr(VI) and MO anionic pollutants removal (Velusamy et al., 2021). There are many adsorbents available, including activated carbon, zeolites, ion exchange resins, metal-organic frameworks, bio-adsorbents, and hydrogels have been utilized to date for heavy metal and dye removal (Fei & Hu, 2023; Bayuo et al., 2023; Niu et al., 2021). In most cases, they still face challenges related to low adsorption capacity, secondary pollution, and costly manufacturing.

Hydrogels are composed of individual chains of a hydrophilic polymer with a high amount of functional groups that are linked chemically or physically together (through hydrogen bonds and electrostatic forces) and stacked in three dimensions (Foudazi et al., 2023; Guo et al., 2020). Due to their high affinity for water, they can hold large amounts of water while maintaining a stable structure in water without compromising structural integrity or strength due to the crosslinking of their polymer chains that prevents them from being solubilized. Using a hydrogel matrix having a surface covered with abundant hydrophilic functional groups and accessible to water, both anionic MO dye and Cr(VI) ions can be electrostatically captured from wastewater, or they can be captured through hydrogen bonding and chelation, thereby achieving the goal of capturing the targeted pollutants. By separating the hydrogel from wastewater, secondary pollution can be prevented because the hydrogel is insoluble and stable in water. Since petroleum-based raw materials utilized to manufacture hydrogel-based adsorbents are usually non-degradable and biologically toxic, biopolymer-derived hydrogels are becoming increasingly popular as adsorbents thanks to their high renewable material content, eco-friendliness, and affordability (Al-Gethami et al., 2024; Radoor et al., 2024). Consequently, the preferred approach to wastewater treatment now involves hydrogels that use abundant and sustainable natural polymers, which are sustainable, biodegradable, and affordable (Al-Gethami et al., 2024).

As representative naturally occurring polymers that contain abundant amounts of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups on their surfaces, respectively, xanthan gum (XG) and chitosan (CS) are both available for use (Fig. S1) (Abu Elella et al., 2021; Harugade et al., 2023). Adsorbents based on them can provide rigid structural support and serve as binding sites for dye molecules and heavy metal ions, and they are also suitable as the “skeletal framework” on which to build hydrogels. The drawbacks of hydrogels made only by physically crosslinking individual natural biopolymers like xanthan gum and chitosan are that they offer insufficient mechanical strength, are unstable in acidic solutions, have only one type of active site, and as adsorbents, possess a limited affinity towards targeted anionic pollutants (Cr (VI) and MO). Therefore, this makes them unsuitable for adsorbing those pollutants (Patel et al., 2020; Shariatinia & Mazloom Jalali, 2018). Accordingly, the development of naturally occurring polymers-derived hydrogels as adsorbents has been attempted in several ways in recent years. These methods include (1) the modification of natural sources-produced polymer chains by grafting (Bhattacharya & Misra, 2004; Maji & Maiti, 2021); (2) incorporating one or more synthetic polymers into formulations (Jin & Bai, 2002); (3) using free radical polymerization to assemble network structures from unsaturated olefinic monomers (Singh et al., 2009); and (4) the introduction cross-linkers, either organic or inorganic small molecules (Zhang et al., 2018). For example, Pb(II) was adsorbable from aqueous solutions using chitosan-g-poly(acrylic acid), attapulgite/sodium humate composite hydrogel and porous lignin-based poly(acrylic acid)/organomontmorillonite nanocomposite that were prepared with MBA as the crosslinking agent (Ma et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2011). Jiang and co-workers studied chitosan grafted with β-cyclodextrin using maleoyl chains to form a bridge, followed by glutaraldehyde cross-linking, and showed that the resulting composite selectively adsorbs methyl orange (MO) from water (Jiang et al., 2018). Sethi et al. reported grafting acrylamide onto chitosan through malic acid chemical cross-linking using a free radical polymerization mechanism and the resulting hydrogel of chitosan crosslinked with malic acid was capable of adsorbing Cr(VI) ions in an aqueous medium (427 mg/g) (Sethi et al., 2022). There has been a report of boosted Cr(VI) adsorption at acid pH using the diepoxyoctane-crosslinked chitosan beads as an adsorbent (Vakili et al., 2018). Hydrogels prepared with synthetic polymers and chemical crosslinkers, however, pose several problems for the environment, including fossil fuel dependency, non-degradability, and biotoxicity. Consequently, it is beneficial to develop eco-friendly adsorbents based on composite or nanocomposite hydrogels rich in adsorption sites that are derived from natural and renewable sources rather than non-renewable synthetic polymers. A great deal of interest is being given to carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and their composite materials as nano-adsorbent materials due to their pronounced physical, chemical, mechanical, and optical characteristics (Sui et al., 2020).

A new rising star in the carbon family, CQDs possess favorable properties due to their aqueous solubility, eco-friendliness, abundance of low-cost sources, abundant functional groups and active sites (such as amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl), high stability, and electron mobility. A CD's band gap makes it suitable to use as a fluorescent nanomaterial. Additionally, the graphite-like cores of CDs and functional groups on their surfaces interact heavily when exposed to heavy metals, causing their fluorescence emissions to become more selectively quenched. In addition to facilitating dye molecule capture, CQDs can trap heavy metal ions effectively, which makes it possible to detect heavy metals in water. A heteroatom doping CQDs like nitrogen has made notable improvements to optical properties as well as adsorption capabilities (Schneider et al., 2017). A number of electron-donating groups are formed on NCQD surfaces after nitrogen-doped groups are added, such as amino and amide groups, as the ethylene diamine is added in the course of synthesis. Since NCQDs have electron-donating groups, they exhibit a greater fluorescence intensity both in solution and in hydrogel systems due to their creation of radiative recombination centers (Dong et al., 2013). A major benefit of hydrogel with the 3D network structure is its ability to immobilize CDs, thereby enabling heavy metal ions to be detected and removed without secondary contamination. By utilizing cellulose nanofibrils and CDs, Guo and colleagues have developed a fluorescent nanocellulose hydrogel as an effective heavy metal ion adsorbent as well as an optical sensor to detect heavy metal ions (Guo et al., 2019). Based on a published study using titanate and carbon dots-modified cellulose nanofibers, it was reported by Luo et al. that fluorescent hydrogels based on chitosan were prepared that adsorb as well as detect heavy metal ions (Luo et al., 2021). The research team of Zhang et al. investigated the fabrication of a fluorescent hydrogel based on starch embedded with CDs nanoparticles and cellulose nanofibrils to simultaneously adsorb and detect Pb(II) (Zhang et al., 2024). According to the research conducted in the lab. of Chowdhury et al., an agarose hydrogel embedded with CDs proved to be an effective way of detecting heavy metals optically and separating them (Gogoi et al., 2015).

The objective of this study was to prepare a bio-based nanocomposite hydrogel derived from the assembly of two naturally occurring biopolymers and NCQD NPs arranged into well-designed structures. Combined with enlarging surface area as well as a rise in functional group abundance, this approach would enhance the absorption performance of individual polymers while simultaneously improving heavy metal ions fluorescence detection. A binary composition containing XG and CS is intriguing in this regard due to their favorable characteristics. A specific pH induces electrostatic bonding between XG and CS as anionic and cationic biopolymers, respectively, forming a blended polymer network (complex hydrogel matrix). Thus, in this study, two biopolymers mentioned above were employed for the skeleton of the hydrogel, incorporating NCQDs to emit fluorescent light. Incorporating NCQD NPs into a 3D network hydrogel of XG and CS resulted in a novel self-assembled nanocomposite hydrogel (XG/CS/NCQDs). The hydrogel nanocomposite serves as an adsorbent for toxic anionically charged MO dye and Cr(VI) in water as well as a fluorescent detector for Cr(VI) in water. The nanocomposite hydrogel was analyzed for its morphology, synthesis mechanism, structure, and properties. In addition, the nanocomposite hydrogel's fluorescent response and the Cr(VI) ion concentration were investigated. NCQDs' effects on structure, swelling behavior, pH sensitivity and Cr(VI) and MO adsorption of the optimized prepared nanocomposite hydrogel sample were investigated and compared. The batch method was used to evaluate the performance of XG/CS/NCQDs to remove Cr(VI) and MO was systematically evaluated under a wide range of adsorption parameters, which includes the adsorption time, pH, temperature, initial Cr(VI) or MO concentrations, and adsorbent dosage. The adsorption mechanism was explored by testing MO and Cr(VI) adsorption isotherms, modeling adsorption kinetics, and analyzing the hydrogel prior to and after the adsorption. An analysis of the nanocomposite hydrogel's reusability was conducted by repeatedly adsorbing and desorbing it. Moreover, the interplay between adsorbent and pollutants was explored as well as the removal mechanisms. To author's knowledge, this study represents the first attempt to fabricate and apply the self-assembled nanocomposite hydrogel as obtained from incorporating NCQD NPs into the polymer network of xanthan gum and chitosan for the detection and removal of polar organic and inorganic pollutants in the aquatic environment.

A domestically produced methyl orange acid dye (MO, C14H14N3NaO3S, molecular weight: 327.32) was used for the study (Mojallali Chemicals Co., Tehran, Iran). It is of analytical grade and has not been purified further. Its maximum absorption wavelength (λmax) was 464 nm. Sigma-Aldrich supplied chitosan has Mw of 190–310 kDa on average, 75–85 % deacetylation, and 200–800 cP viscosity was used in this study. Anhydrous citric acid (CA, (99 %, C6H8O7) and ethylene diamine (EDA), ≥99 %) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Xanthan gum (XG) with a Brookfield viscosity of 1717 cps (in 1 % KCl) and a molecular weight about 2 × 106 g/mol was obtained from Alhavi Pharmaceuticals Co. (Tehran, Iran). A supplier of ethanol (>99.8 %) was Kimia Alcohol Company (Zanjan, Iran). All analytical grade metal salts of cadmium chloride monohydrate (CdCl2.H2O), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), copper sulphate pentahydrate (CuSO4.5H2O), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), mercuric chloride (HgCl2), manganese(II) chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2.4H2O), calcium chloride CaCl2 and so on were all purchased from Merck. These metal salts were used as received. All solutions were prepared with distilled water.

Hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) was conducted using ethylene diamine as a co-reagent and citric acid as a precursor (Zhu et al., 2013). The formation of carbon dots is triggered by the condensation reaction of citric acid and ethylene diamine with functionalization, doping, and subsequent carbonization producing polymer-like CDs that turn into NCQDs. Briefly, 1.0507 g of citric acid and 335 μl of ethylene diamine were dissolved in distilled water (10 mL). The solution was then poured into an autoclave lined with Teflon (50 mL), sealed, and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. Upon completing the reaction, the reactor was naturally cooled to room temperature. A microporous membrane (pore size 0.22 μm) was used to filter the obtained solution (in brown-black and transparent) to separate big particles, followed by 36 h of dialysis (using a dialysis bag) to remove reaction residues, by-products, and purify the CDs aqueous suspension. To obtain a dry solid NCQDs sample, the as prepared a light brown aqueous NCQDs suspension was powdered by freeze-drying.

By using an eco-friendly aqueous solution casting procedure, hydrogels loaded with different concentrations of NCQDs nanoparticles were prepared under different conditions. In brief, 0.4 g of dry chitosan was dissolved into a beaker containing of 2 % (w/v) acetic acid solution on a magnetic stirrer and kept to fully swell and obtain a homogeneous solution. In another beaker containing 7 mL of water, 0.1 g of xanthan was dissolved. NCQDs was then added at ratios of 1 % to 4 % to the above chitosan solution at pH = 6 and ultrasonically agitated for 30 min to promote their complete nanoparticles dispersion. Afterward, the two homogeneous solutions are slowly mixed at 25 °C for 30 min, while continuously stirring. After removing the air bubbles by sonication, a homogeneous gel was formed. The next step involved immersing the hydrogels in ethanol for 12 h and distilled water for 24 h while exchanging water every 8 h to remove any remaining constituent residues, after which the obtained XG/CS/NCQDs were freeze-dried for 48 h. Finally, the XG/CS/NCQDs sample was now ready for further analysis after drying in a vacuum oven for 24 h at 50 °C.

Applied characterization techniques and other methods including swelling ratio measurement, adsorption study procedure, reuse of spent adsorbent, Cr(VI) and MO removal kinetic and isotherm analysis, adsorption thermodynamic study, Cr(VI) ion fluorescent sensing procedure, and determination of the point of zero charge are provided in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of novel XG/CS/NCQDs hydrogel biofilm
ce:section.ce:para: For verification of the potential interactions between NCQDs and XG/CS framework and whether final nanocomposite was formed, an FT-IR analysis of XG/CS/NCQDs and XG/CS synthesized hydrogels, as well as their constituent materials (NCQDs, CS, and XG), is depicted in Fig. 1 . In neat XG, the OH stretching vibrations led to a wide absorbance band around 3440 cm−1, whereas moderate absorption bands can be seen at 2917 and 2868 cm−1 associated with the stretching vibrations of CH. In particular, the absorption peaks of 1731, 1625, and 1412 cm−1 are due to vibrations associated with the -C=O bond of -COOH as well as the -COOH asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations (Rahmatpour & Alizadeh Hesarsorkh, 2024; Thakur et al., 2017). In a region of around 1050 cm−1, there was a peak caused by the acetal stretching vibrations (CH2-O-CH2). The CS spectrum showed a band of absorption at about 3453 cm−1 that overlapped with vibrations associated with stretching of -OH and -NH2. Absorption peaks have been observed at 1658 cm−1 (vibrations due to stretching the -C=O) and at 1591 cm−1 (vibrations caused by NH2 bending), respectively, due to amide (I) and amide (II) stretching vibrations (Rahmatpour & Alizadeh Hesarsorkh, 2024; Jiang et al., 2018). The -CH vibration peaks were also identified at 1380 cm−1 for bending, 2914 cm−1 for asymmetric stretching, and 2870 cm−1 for symmetric stretching. There are also small peaks at 1085 and 1015 cm−1 that are related to the vibrations caused by the stretching of CO. In the case of NCQDs (Fig. 1), the absorption band is a broad one at 3435 cm−1, which is caused by stretching vibrations in NH and OH bonds. Thus, NCQDs contain alcohol (-OH) and amine (−NH2) groups. Two bands appeared at 1665 cm−1 and 1454 cm−1, one related to the stretching of the CO bond and the other related to the stretching of the carboxylic acid's CO bond (Tadesse et al., 2018; Liu, Cui, et al., 2019). A pair of vibrations have been identified while overlapping the absorption band at 1580 cm−1, one being a stretching vibration of CC, and the other being a bending vibration of NH. The C-OH and C-N = bonds exhibit stretching vibration peaks at 1046 and 1390 cm−1, respectively. Further, a small peak was identified at 1769 cm−1, which was attributed to the carboxylic acid present within NCQDs. The results revealed that the pyrolysis of citric acid yielded N-doped CQDs that contained high levels of functional groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on their surfaces, suggesting that NCQDs were successfully synthesized. The FT-IR analysis also revealed that the synthesized NCQDs contained amino groups on their surfaces, which significantly contributed to their colloidal stability in water. Bio-nanocomposite hydrogel film composed of XG and CS preserved the vibration mode of -C=O stretching (acetate groups) formerly observed in XG but displayed an absorption with less intensity around 1726 cm−1. It suggests that gelation via complexation does not affect ester groups. In examining the IR spectra of XG/CS hydrogel with and without NCQDs (Fig. 2a), NCQDs interaction with CS and XG chains is evident by shifting the characteristic peaks.

The XRD patterns (Fig. 1) illustrate the crystalline structure of the prepared hydrogel XG/CS/NCQDs with and without NPs, and their constituents (XG, CS, and CQDs). Based on XG's XRD profile there is a distinctive diffraction peak around 2θ = 19.5°, indicating that it is characteristically amorphous (Rahmatpour & Alizadeh Hesarsorkh, 2024; Makhado et al., 2018). Neat CS demonstrated a pronounced diffraction peak at about 2θ =20.2°, indicating crystalline regions. Two peaks of small diffraction were observed at around 2θ = 29.4° and 26.3°, providing evidence that amorphous regions existed (Rahmatpour & Alizadeh Hesarsorkh, 2024; Chen et al., 2023). In view of the extensive hydrogen bonds (both inter- and intramolecular) forming among hydroxyl and amino groups in the repeating unit of the CS structure, a peak of diffraction at 2θ = 20.2° can be ascribed to the arrangement of hydrated and anhydrous crystallographic regions. A broad diffraction peak with low intensity occurred near 2θ = 21.4°, proving that the carbon in the quantum dots was predominantly amorphous and further confirming that NCQDs possessed graphite-like carbon cores (Chen et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2013). There is a broad and weak peak at nearly the same location near 2θ = 21.90° shown in the XG/CS hydrogel diffractogram. Due to an electrostatic interaction between XG and CS in this case, hydrogen bonds between the molecules are broken. Meanwhile, crystalline disruption occurs in both polysaccharides, particularly CS, which results in the loss of crystallinity degree in the hybrid XG/CS hydrogel. Chitosan's characteristic diffraction peak in the XRD spectrum of XG/CS hydrogel no longer exists or at least is less intense in intensity. In comparison to the XG/CS gel, the XG/CS/NCQDs nanocomposite gel presented a similar X-ray diffractogram. However, compared to the XG/CS gel, it had a lesser degree of crystallinity with a broadening of its peak, which indicated a lower degree of crystallinity. Consequently, incorporating NCQDs into a network of XG and CS, and then gelation induced by dense non-covalent hydrogen bonds between the multifunctional NCQD NPs together with CS and XG, significantly altered the crystallographic structure of both CS and XG as well as the nanoparticles they contained. There was also a weakening of the prominent CS and XG peaks around 10° and 20° in the nanocomposite, indicating that chitosan, XG, and NCQDs interacted strongly.

TG/DTG analyses were conducted on XG/CS/NCQDs and precursor samples to determine how they behaved thermally, as depicted in Fig. 1c and d. Between 183 and 310 °C, significant and minor stages of weight loss were observed on the DTG curve of XG/CS/NCQDs. According to the findings, at 245 °C there was a substantial weight loss caused by the concurrent decomposition of CS and XG. However, XG decomposition starts a bit earlier and is completed earlier CS and XG powders, XG/CS, and XG/CS/NCQDs were decomposed at about 310 °C, 316 °C, 249° and 250 °C, respectively. The decomposition temperature of XG/CS and XG/CS/NCQDs is lower than the respective XG and CS precursors. In agreement with the FTIR and XRD results, it is confirmed that strong interactions were found between the components of the composite and nanocomposite hydrogel. There is a possibility that the physical form of both polymer samples may contribute to decreased thermal stability (; Habiba et al., 2017). The nanocomposite hydrogel left about 33 % residue at 800 °C.

To ascertain whether aromatic carbons were present in the synthesized NCQDs, the Raman spectrum was recorded (Fig. 1e). As can be seen from NCQD's Raman spectrum, a characteristic peak is identified at 1319 cm−1 and another at 1522 cm−1, originating from non-crystalline D-band (sp3) and crystalline G-band (sp2), respectively. According to common belief, both the D-band and the G-band are associated with the carbon atom's vibration mode with the sp3 hybridized orbital and the sp2 hybridized orbital, respectively (Du et al., 2023). Based on the relative area intensity (ID/IG) of the D and G bands, the percentage of sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms was calculated as 1.07. Based on this value, the NCQDs prepared are composed of two types of carbon species in comparable proportions. A Raman analysis of the XG/CS/NCQDs nanocomposite hydrogel failed to reveal any obvious characteristic peaks of NCQDs. This may be due to high fluorescence that interferes with Raman characterization (Fig. S1). For NCQDs prepared with CA bio-precursor and EDA co-reagent as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively a dynamic light scattering (DLS) study was also conducted. The DLS histogram shown in Fig. 1f illustrates the sizes of freshly prepared NCQDs. A size of 3.15 nm was determined on average.

As illustrated in Fig. 2, FE-SEM images depict the morphology of the network structure of both the XG/CS and XG/CS/NCQDs materials. Fig. 2a and b illustrates that they both consisted of three-dimensional networks. In the hydrogel that consisted of XG and CS, the network appeared continuous, but pores were few, and its texture appeared uneven and compact, and it did not look homogeneous (Fig. 2a). The physical crosslinking between the chains of both biopolymers accounts for forming the network structure that causes morphological changes. NCQDs' crystal lattices could not be observed since NCQDs were wrapped in the hydrogel matrix XG/CS, which had a porous structure (Fig. 2b). Although the 3D network structure of the hydrogel remained intact, it had become more porous and smaller. An image taken using TEM of the as-prepared NCQDs dispersed in water revealed dot-like morphology and roundish dots of varying sizes ranging from 0.5 to 4.0 nm, corresponding to a 2.25 nm diameter distribution on average (Fig. 2c). In the TEM images shown in (Fig. 2d, e), NCQDs are embedded within the XG/CS/NCQDs hydrogel matrix and appear well dispersed without aggregating. However, TEM failed to detect NCQDs in XG/CS/NCQDs gel because of interference caused by the dense gel structure, and only faint shadows of suspended dot-like NCQDs were visible. A Raman spectrum of XG/CS/NCQDs hydrogel did not reveal any obvious D or G bands, supporting the hypothesis (Fig. S2). Based on the TEM image, Fig. 2f is a histogram showing the diameter distribution of synthesized NCQDs.

Taking into account the results of the characterization section, Scheme 1 represents schematically how fluorescent XG/CS/NCQDs nanocomposite hydrogel is formed. The inclusion of NCQDs as a functional nanomaterial into a XG/CS gelling network was achieved through a simple solution casting technique, which produced a XG/CS/NCQDs self-assembled fluorescent nanocomposite hydrogel naturally devoid of the need for cross-linkers. Having been dissolved in dilute acetic acid, chitosan contains amino groups (-NH3 +) capable of interacting physically with xanthan carboxyl groups (-CO2 −), and the hydrogel network was formed as a result. In the XG/CS/NCQDs, CS and XG build the main framework, and they are accountable for dispersing NCQD NPs stably. The XG/CS is a polymer blended network capable of developing 3D porous structures as a natural framework, thus providing channels for mass transfer. Additionally, NCQDs embedded in the XG/CS matrix functioned as fluorescent probes for detecting Cr(VI) ions. Meanwhile, NCQD NPs enhanced the surface area of chitosan + xanthan gum-based self-assembled hydrogel for dye and metal ion adsorption by modifying the network structure of the hydrogel. NCQD NPs composed of carboxylic acid, hydroxyl, and amino groups allowed strong hydrogen bonding interactions to occur between NCQDs and the XG and CS matrix due to both biopolymers having -OH groups. It is due to the hydrophilic characteristics of XG, CS, and NCQDs that strong interactions and interlocking can occur, forming the nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanocomposite hydrogels with enhanced swelling capacities

ce:section.ce:para: In some control experiments without NCQD nanoparticles, we tested whether blending XG and CS at varying ratios would result in a gel (Table S1, entry 17). It was found that mixed solutions of CS in dilute acetic acid and XG in water under identical conditions resulted in a homogeneous solution rather than a self-assembled hydrogel. Gel formation occurred when a salt such as sodium chloride at a certain concentration was added to the homogenous XG/CS mixture due to screening the charges on the XG and CS polyelectrolytes with salt. In the absence of NCQD NPs, a hydrogel's ability to self-assemble is inhibited (Table S1). Using hydrogels to remove pollutants from aqueous media is dependent on their swelling degree. The aim was to obtain XG/CS/CQDs nanocomposite hydrogel that has the highest swelling level, and for this, we searched for its optimal preparation conditions. A combination of constituent ratios, temperature, time, and pH were optimized to achieve a XG/CS/NCQDs nanocomposite gel with the highest water uptake capacity. To optimize the preparation conditions for nanocomposite XG/CS/NCQDs hydrogels, the XG:CS weight ratio was varied as well as NCQDs were incorporated into the XG/CS matrix at varying proportions (Table S1). Both (XG and CS) were analyzed in the ratios of 1:1 (XG: CS) to 1:6 and 1:1 to 1:8 (CS: XG). Our findings showed that the hydrogel swelling percentage changed depending on the ratio at which it was formed. Hydrogel formation is prevented by an excess of the same kind of reactant and an insufficient amount of physical interactions between polymeric chains. A ratio of 1:4 was found to be optimal for XG/CS and a percentage of NCQDs of 3 (wt%) was found to be optimal for NCQDs as well. Moreover, the swelling property was examined while maintaining other variables constant while changing the PH of the reaction medium (Fig. S4c (SI)). Based on Table S1, a pH-dependent swelling behavior was observed in hydrogels. In the optimal sample of formulated XG/CS/NCQDs hydrogel (Table S1, no. 3), ESR rose rapidly from 128 to 328 g/g at pH 2.0–6.0 and then fell from 295 to 99 g/g between pH 7.0 and 8.0. As illustrated in Fig. S4c, between pH 2.0 and 6.0, a significant increase in swelling ratio was observed, which declined between pH 7.0 and 8.0, indicating that the nanocomposite hydrogel biofilm swelled greatest at pH 6.0. A pH increase from 4.0 to 6.0 led to a gradual change in carboxyl groups in the hydrogels from -COOH to -COO−. XG/CS/NCQDs hydrogel exhibits a pH-responsive behavior primarily due to pyruvic and acetic acid residues in the XG side chains. In the side chains of XG, which is a naturally anionic polyelectrolyte, there are many carboxyl groups (-CO2 −) (Nsengiyumva & Alexandridis, 2022), which are protonated into -CO2H in highly acidic conditions. Most carboxyl groups in the XGs (pKa = 4.6) and NCQDs components protonated and existed in the form of -COOH groups under acidic conditions, causing hydrogen bond interactions, inter-polymer associations, and more bonding to develop between them. The electrostatic repulsion between -COO− groups is inhibited by this, resulting in compacting and shrinking of the network structure and leading to a lower swelling percentage. With a rise in external pH from 2.0 to 6.0, the ionization of the -COOH groups intensified electrostatic repulsion, causing the hydrogel network to expand and further swell. Consequently, pH (6.0) resulted in optimal swelling. However, from pH 6.0 to 9.0, the carboxyl groups on XG and NCQDs were deprotonated, causing a charge screening effect mediated by counter ions (Na+) that shielded the electrostatic repulsion between the molecular chains thereby preventing swelling. Nanocomposite hydrogel behaves differently in response to pH due to pH-dependent swelling. To evaluate how temperature affected the water absorption in the prepared nanocomposites hydrogel network, we varied the reaction mixture temperature between 25 and 65 °C, while other variables were held constant throughout testing (Table S1). A temperature of 45 °C resulted in a maximum ESR (Table S1). A decrease in ESR was observed when the temperature exceeded 45 °C. The physical interactions between functional groups of two main biopolymer components and NCQD nanoparticles weaken as temperatures rise, preventing the self-assembly of a hydrogel network. The amount of solvent volume affects the ESR of prepared nanocomposite material. Increased solvent usage during preparation traps water within the nanocomposite matrix, decreasing the tendency to absorb water and decreasing the swelling percentage. Ten mL of solvent proved to be the optimal amount.

The unique characteristic of the prepared nanocomposite hydrogel is its capability to retain water or to expand in an aqueous medium without completely deteriorating. This is a critical indicator of how easily and quickly water penetrates the hydrogel matrix, determining the absorption rate. The curves in Fig. S4a (SI) illustrate the swelling kinetics of the prepared hydrogels with and without NCQDs in distilled water. As can be seen, the swelling ratio for the hydrogel without NCQDs NPs was conspicuously lower than that of the hydrogel containing NCQDs NPs at the same time. The ESR of XG/CS/NCQDs and XG/CS optimal hydrogel samples in distilled water under optimal preparation conditions was 328 and 98 g/g, respectively (Table S1, entries 3,17). While both samples with and without NCQDs demonstrated comparable swelling patterns, they were different in their hydration levels, and the XG/CS/NCQDs sample adsorbed water faster than the sample without NCQDs at the beginning of the swelling phase according to Fig. S4a (SI). For XG/CS/NCQDs gel (Fig. S4a, SI), the absorption of water increased rapidly within the first 30 min (0–30 min), followed by a slow fall until plateauing (80 min). The hydrogel network based on XG and CS had a water uptake capacity of 9800 %. The water uptake capacity of the optimized sample is almost quadrupled by the incorporation of NCQD NPs. A rise in swelling percentage could be caused by the uniform distribution of NCQD NPs within the hydrogel network, contributing to a greater surface area and porosity as well as the electrostatic interactions. A hydrogel XG/CS/NCQDs became saturated faster than a hydrogel XG/CS in <80 min after adding NCQDs. As shown in Fig. S4b (SI), compared to XG/CS, the swelling ratio increased with incorporating NCQDs into the hydrogel network and then decreased when it exceeded 3 wt%. The reason could be that as NCQDs content increases, more hydrophilic groups, especially carboxyl groups, would be introduced into the hydrogel, thereby facilitating water diffusion and improving the water absorption capability. This leads to increasing XG/CS/NCQDs hydrogel swelling ratio along with NCQDs content increasing to 3 wt%. As it turned out, N-doped CQDs surfaces are rich in hydrophilic groups, including hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl, which tend to be strongly hydrophilic. Additionally, adding NCQDs to the mixture resulted in a porous structure, which increased the surface area, leading to enhanced swelling ability. However, the equilibrium swelling capacity decreased when the amount of NCQDs exceeded 3 wt%. A crosslinking process may be responsible for this finding by forming more bonds since NCQDs serve as multi-crosslinkers and therefore, the movement of polymer chains in a highly crosslinked network would be severely hindered. Rigid hydrogels are formed by shrinking the pore sizes and reducing the spacing between chains, thereby resisting expansion and swelling. Studies on the swelling of cellulose/carboxymethyl cellulose/clay hydrogels reported by Peng et al. and PVA/carboxymethyl cellulose/GO hydrogels reported by Dai et al. showed similar results (Dai et al., 2018; Peng et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of XG/CS/NCQDs nanocomposite gel film
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the fluorescence XG/CS/NCQDs gel were recorded to identify its optical properties. It can be observed in Fig. 3 a that the UV–vis spectra of NCQDs and XG/CS/NCQDs gel dispersed in distilled water exhibit shoulder peaks around 235 nm, which are associated with the π-π* transition of the nuclei of NCQDs' conjugated structure (sp2 aromatic carbon domains) and their carbonyl groups (C=C/C=O) (Du et al., 2023; Yuan et al., 2021). An additional absorption band near 340 nm comes from the n-π* transition of functional groups on NCQDs surface containing nitrogen or oxygen as well as CS and XG that possess functional groups containing nitrogen and oxygen (-NH2, -CO2H). It is evident that since XG and CS' carboxyl and amine groups interact electrostatically with NCQDs' surface groups, these interactions interfere with the NCQD's surface group absorption of energy, resulting in a significantly lower peak intensity for the hydrogel than for NCQDs in solution. Additionally, Fig. 3a depicts the PL emission and excitation spectra of XG/CS/NCQDs. We found that the optimal emission wavelength (λem) was 427 nm, while maximum excitation (λex) occurred at 360 nm. The presence of one λex indicates that there is one type of excitation energy trapped on the NCQDs. As a result, the subsequent fluorescence experiment was conducted at 360 nm as the excitation wavelength. As mentioned above, by incorporating NCQDs NPs into the polymeric matrix, the nanocomposite hydrogel film displays PL properties. A PL property of the nanocomposite hydrogel film is shown in Fig. S4d (SI). The diluted aqueous NCQDs dispersion did not show any sign of precipitation after three months at ambient temperature (Fig. 3a, inset: left). As shown by the inset at right, NCQDs are optically and colloidally stable, emitting intense blue luminescence under 365 nm UV light. In Fig. 3b, NCQDs dispersed in aqueous solution are excited by light of varying wavelengths (λex: 310, 320, 330, 340, 350, 360, 370, 380, 390, 400 nm) and their PL emission spectra are recorded. It is noteworthy that PL emission peaks shifted slightly when the wavelength of excitation (λex) ranged between 310 and 400 nm, providing evidence that NCQDs possess specific PL characteristics, as opposed to the previously reported NCQDs having excitation wavelength-dependent PL characteristics (Zhu et al., 2013). We observed maximum wavelengths of 360 nm and 427 in excitation and emission, respectively. Having the excitation-independent property makes NCQDs potentially useful since autofluorescence can be avoided. Fig. 3d displays the PL spectra of XG/CS/NCQDs gel excited with the wavelengths indicated above. According to our findings, λ about 360 nm and 427 nm were the optimal excitation and emission bands, respectively. An important difference exists between NCQDs dispersed in distilled water and XG/CS/NCQDs-gel in terms of PL emission wavelength and intensity; however, the emission band-width of XG/CS/NCQDs-gel is somewhat greater compared with that of NCQDs in solution. Fig. 3d demonstrates that by increasing the excitation wavelength (λex) from 310 to 400 nm, the corresponding emission peak wavelength (λem) of XG/CS/NCQDs gel moved towards longer wavelengths (low energy), indicative of a bathochromic shift (red shift) from 383 to 470 nm. There was a slight excitation wavelength dependence in the emission spectrum of NCQDs in solution, however this dependence was stronger in XG/CS/NCQDs gel emission spectrum. XG/CS/NCQDs gel exhibited a slightly wider fluorescence emission peak than the NCQDs in solution (Fig. 3d). The variations in wavelength and intensity of PL emission of XG/CS/NCQDs gel or its excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior may be due to nitrogen-doped groups existing on the NCQDs' surface, altering the surface and defect states, which consequently affect the bandgap of CNQDs (Ding et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2017). When EDA is added to the synthesis process, the CQDs surface modification occurs by forming abundant electron-donating groups, such as amino groups (-NH2) and amide groups (-NCO-). When irradiated with a UV lamp (λex = 365 nm), XG/CS/NCQDs gel shows a very bright blue fluorescence. According to Fig. 4a, there are changes in fluorescence appearance in XG/CS/NCQD-0, XG/CS/NCQD-10, XG/CS/NCQD-20, XG/CS/NCQD-60, XG/CS/NCQD-100, XG/CS/NCQD-150, and XG/CS/NCQD-200. According to the observed fluorescence, the most intense fluorescence was observed for XG/CS/CQD-100. A 59.7 % absolute quantum yield (AQY) was measured for XG/CS/CQD-100. In the Supplementary material, we describe how to measure AQY. Hence, the XG/CS/CQD-100 hydrogel had the potential for fluorescent probe and all other experiments were performed with this.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cr(VI) ion detection performance of novel XG/CS/NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: By fluorescence quenching of NCQDs enclosed in this novel 3D porous fluorescent nanocomposite hydrogel, this novel optical sensor can detect Cr (VI). It was explored whether the XG/CS/NCQDs gel could be used to detect Cr(VI) ions as a solid probe. The XG/CS/CQD-100 hydrogel was tested for its ability to detect Cr(VI) ions in the presence of various metal ions, as well as its selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Cr(VI) ions. As such, we determined the extent to which the XG/CS/NCQDs gel is selective for Cr (VI) by measuring the fluorescence quenching capacity of several heavy metal ions at 2000 mg/L, such as Cu2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, K+, Zn2+, Na+, Li+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Cr6+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Mn2+. Fig. 4b demonstrates Cr(VI) ions had the highest impact on the fluorescence quenching of XG/CS/CQDs-100 gel in comparison to the other tested metal ions, whereas XG/CS/NCQDs-100 gel did not exhibit any noticeable fluorescence quenching when exposed to the other mentioned metal ions. That means when other metal ions exist in the concentration of 2000 mg/L, the fluorescence quenching triggered by Cr(VI) did not change, and its coexistence with other metal ions had negligible impact. XG/CS/NCQDs-100 gel exhibited high selectivity towards Cr(VI) ions, as demonstrated in the results described above. Furthermore, an analysis of the F/F0 of XG/CS/NCQDs-100 after exposure to the metal ions mentioned above showed that it did not significantly reduce, except for Cr(VI) (Fig. 4c). The fluorescence behavior of XG/CS/NCQDs-100 hydrogel was completely quenched by Cr(VI) as opposed to the weak influence of Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, and Ca2+ metal ions. Additional evidence in support of nanocomposite hydrogel's outstanding selectivity as a Cr(VI) sensor was provided by the findings outlined here. Chromium ion (Cr+6) shows the highest amount of fluorescence quenching, indicating a significant interaction between Cr+6 and the NCQDs in the gel formulation. Cr(VI) ion strongly quenches the fluorescence of XG/CS/NCQDs that contain functional groups containing nitrogen and oxygen, particularly carboxyl and amino groups in NCQDs. The functional groups in NCQDs are capable of high affinity to Cr(VI) ions, and these functional groups in NCQDs formed a complex with Cr(VI) ions. This complex formation caused the electrons from the excited state of NCQDs to be transferred to the d-orbitals of Cr(VI) ions; consequently, since Cr(VI) ions were capable of capturing electrons, some electrons were prevented from reaching NCQDs, contributing to a quench of fluorescence (Wang et al., 2016, Sarkar et al., 2018; Liu, Chen, et al., 2019). Nanocomposite hydrogels' sensitivity to Cr(VI) detection (i.e. Cr(VI) concentration) has been further explored. With the aim of determining how the XG/CS/NCQDs-100 gel responds by changing Cr(VI) concentrations, several standard solutions that contain Cr(VI) concentrations ranging from 0 to 200 μM were mixed with XG/CS/NCQDs-100 gel. With increasing Cr(VI) concentration, the PL fluorescent intensity of the XG/CS/NCQDs-100 gel dispersion declined gradually when excited with a UV lamp at 365 nm wavelength (Fig. 4d). It was evident that there was a strong fluorescence response towards Cr(VI) ions. According to Fig. 5d, XG/CS/NCQDs fluorescence intensity decreased by >25 % when submerged in a solution of Cr(VI) ions equal to 100 μM. A reduction in F/F0 of XG/CS/NCQDs-100 of 100 % to 32 % was observed within a given range of Cr(VI) concentrations. It was observed that relative fluorescence quenching intensity (F/F0) and concentration of (VI) ions are positively correlated (R2 = 0.9929) (Fig. 4d). Based on the calibration curve, a linear range equation can be expressed as F/F0 = a[Cr(VI)] + b. We found that the Cr(VI) ion can be detected at a limit of 0.29 μM using the XG/CS/NCQDs. There was a linear range with concentrations ranging from 1 to 100 μM for which the regression equation was F/F0 = −0.0002527 × C0 + 0.9845, where C0 denotes Cr(VI) concentration and 0.9929 was obtained for the R2, suggesting that F/F0 and concentration of Cr(VI) are significantly correlated. It was estimated that the detection limit for Cr(VI) was 0.29 μM based on the 3σ rule (σ = S0/S, where S0 represents the standard deviation calculated after repeating the analysis and S signifies the calibration curve slope).

Additionally, XG/CS/NCQDs-100 was compared to previous work's findings for detecting Cr(VI) (linear range, LOD) (Table 1 ). This novel CS + XG-based fluorescent nanocomposite hydrogel possesses an extensive linearity range and a high limit of detection, probably due to its high adsorption capability. As a result, the detection performance is well suited for detecting Cr(VI) ions in drinking water (0.05 mg.L−1). Moreover, Cr(VI) ions are capable of quenching fluorescent quickly. Over the first 35 s, XG/CS/NCQDs emission intensity rapidly declined, reaching equilibrium within 65 s, indicating its potential to detect Cr(VI) ions rapidly (not shown).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applicability of XG/CS/NCQDs film for removing Cr(VI) and MO
ce:section.ce:para: An optimization study was conducted to identify the optimal conditions for maximizing Cr(VI) and MO anion removal using the XG/CS/NCQDs gel. A visual representation of the Cr(VI) and MO adsorption processes is shown in Fig. 5f. In the first step, MO and K2Cr2O7 solutions (50 mg/L) were prepared separately in two beakers, and two pieces of prepared XG/CS/NCQDs hydrogel (50 mg) were then poured into the beakers under continuous stirring for 100 min. Initially, the solutions were yellow and red-orange, respectively but turned nearly clear as they progressed and two anionic species were adsorbed. According to the findings, the XG/CS/NCQDs hydrogel was effective in adsorbing both MO and Cr(VI) anionic pollutants.

The optimal adsorbent dose was determined by suspending different quantities of XG/CS/NCQDs nanocomposite (10–200 mg) in MO and Cr(VI) solutions in the concentration of 50 mg/L. As represented in Fig. 5a, the adsorbent's dosage (10–200 mg) is plotted against its ability to adsorb and its efficiency of removal. The Cr(VI) adsorption efficiency was observed to rise from 59.2 % to 97.5 %, and from 68 % to 99.7 % for MO when its dosage grew from 10 mg to 50 mg. The best removal efficiency was found with a dosage of 50 mg of the adsorbent. In the presence of CS/XG/NCQDs in greater amounts, more binding unoccupied vacancies and greater surface area are accessible for a given amount of Cr(VI) and MO anions. A constant removal percentage resulted from adsorbent doses exceeding 50 mg, however. As adsorbent doses increase over 50 mg, its adsorption efficiency increases as the nanocomposite gel contains more effective adsorption sites. The results showed, however, that the adsorption ability declined as the adsorbent quantity was raised. At 10 mg and 200 mg adsorbent dosages, maximum and minimum adsorption capacities were obtained (343 and 296 mg/g), respectively. There is an aggregation or overlapping of nanocomposite particles, which reduces the efficient surface area of CS/XG/NCQDs, which results in a decreased adsorption capacity with higher doses. At higher concentrations, diffusion paths increase, causing adsorption to be retarded as well. In subsequent experiments, 50 mg of nanocomposite dosage was found to be the most effective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of Cr(VI) and MO initial concentration
ce:section.ce:para: We tested five pollutant solutions in varying concentrations (10, 50, 100, 200, and 300 mg/L) to assess what is the CS/XG/NCQDs biofilm adsorption capacity and removal efficiency for MO and Cr(VI). Experiments under batch conditions were carried out under optimal conditions (e.g., a 50 mg adsorbent, 25 °C, and pH 2 and 4 for Cr(VI) and MO. According to Fig. 5b, the removal percentages of Cr(VI) and MO (from 100 % to 48 % and 77.6 %) decreased with rising the initial concentrations, and the decrease was accompanied by an increase in the ability of CS/XG/NCQDs to adsorb Cr(VI) and MO (20.41 mg/g to 290 and 26.7 to 452 mg/g). When Cr(VI) and MO are present at low concentrations, most of the active sites on the adsorbent are free enough to bind only small amounts of each pollutant. When MO and Cr(VI) concentrations were initially high, nonetheless, the adsorbent's limited adsorption sites (at a fixed dosage) became easily occupied, making dye molecules and Cr(VI) ions removal more difficult. As a result, this leads to a reduced percentage of MO and Cr(VI) removal due to the increasing amount of both pollutants contained in the solution. Increasing MO and Cr(VI) concentrations would, however, provide an enhanced driving force capable of overcoming the mass transfer resistance of two species across a solid-aqueous interface, resulting in greater adsorption capacity with a rise in pollutant concentrations from 10 to 300 mg/L.

Experiments on XG/CS/NCQD uptake of MO and Cr(VI) under various pH conditions were carried out at pH values between 2 and 10, and respective curves are depicted in Fig. 5c. As the solution pH changed from 2 to 10, both MO and Cr(VI) percentage removal decreased continuously. This downward trend arises from the electrostatic interaction between the XG/CS/NCQDs adsorbent and the adsorbates. The isoelectric point (IEP) or pHzpc of XG/CS/CQDs was found to be 5.80 (Fig. 5d). In a situation where the pH of a solution falls below the IEP of XG/CS/NCQDs, the surface becomes positively charged and MO and Cr(VI) anionic species in solutions become attracted to the surface. A rise in solution pH, decreases XG/CS/NCQDs' positive charge, while the negative charge grows. Consequently, as pH increases, the adsorption is less efficient because both anionic adsorbates and the adsorbed material exhibit weak electrostatic attraction. In the case of MO, the diazo bond (N atom) in the vicinity of the sulfonic acid group of the MO molecule undergoes protonation at pH <3.30 (Fig. S3). In response to the release of the positive charge, the dimethylamine group undergoes a conversion to the conjugated hydroquinone structure, which causes the color to change (Rahmatpour & Alizadeh Hesarsorkh, 2024; Wong et al., 2019). A pH increase of 2 to 4.0 increased MO removal efficiency by the XG/CS/NCQDs film from 90 to 99.7 %. At pH as low as 2.0, the amino and carboxylate groups on CS and XG undergo protonation, and MO in neutral or alkaline solutions occurs as sodium sulfonate (pka = 3.30). A faster rate of MO removal was observed at less acidic pH values (pH > pka). A pH value of 4.0 results in optimal and maximal MO removal (99.7 %), while increasing pH values to a basic level causes dye removal to gradually decline. When the pH becomes basic, excessive OH− ions begin to accumulate and ions start screening out, leading to a rise in OH−/MO competition to bind on the surface of XG/CS/NCQDs, reducing MO interaction and lowering removal efficiency. In regards to Cr(VI) adsorbate, the HCrO4 −, CrO4 2−, and Cr2O7 2− forms exist in aqueous solutions (Deng et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2017). The presence of CrO4 2− is generally observed at pH levels over 6. HCrO4 − and Cr2O7 2− constitute the majority of Cr(VI) between pH 2 and 6. Having a smaller pH gives rise to more sites for adsorption and increased adsorption capability since Cr(VI) adsorption is mainly influenced by electrostatic attraction between the adsorbent and Cr(VI) anions. pH 2.0 and 4.0 resulted in the highest removal percentage for Cr(VI) and MO, respectively. Given these results, the optimum interactions were observed at the pHs mentioned and accordingly, pH levels of 2.0 and 4.0, respectively were used for the adsorption experiments with Cr(VI) and MO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cr(VI) and MO removal kinetics and contact time
ce:section.ce:para: Kinetic experiments were undertaken to estimate the duration of the XG/CS/NCQDs adsorbent should remain exposed to two anionic pollutants, based on their adsorption rates. Several contact times were tested to determine MO and Cr(VI) concentrations per gram of adsorbent (qt) and plotted to evaluate time-dependent adsorption of MO and Cr(VI) by XG/CS/NCQDs nanocomposite. The adsorption kinetic curves of MO and Cr(VI) are displayed in Fig. 5e. In two kinetic curves, it is evident that uptake occurs rapidly during the early stages of contact, but as time goes on, the uptake slows down, and adsorption eventually reaches equilibrium. The reason for this is that there are many adsorption sites that are unoccupied in the initial stages, thereby them readily accessible to Cr(VI) ions and MO molecules for rapid occupancy. A decreasing number of vacant sites remain as the adsorption process progresses, and adsorbate molecules repel each other on an adsorbent's surface as well as within a bulk solution. Moreover, due to the initial gradient in concentration of Cr(VI) and MO pollutants between the bulk solution and interface, all adsorbates will be pushed forward at the interface, leading to a greater uptake rate initially. It took approximately 60 and 120 min for MO and Cr(VI) to reach the adsorption equilibrium onto the XG/CS/NCQDs. The maximum concentration of MO adsorbed after achieving equilibrium adsorption was ca. 99.7 mg/g, whereas the maximum concentration of Cr(VI) adsorbed after reaching equilibrium adsorption was ca. 97.5 mg/g. While both anionic pollutants adsorb to XG/CS/NCQDs nanocomposite with comparable equilibrium adsorption quantities, the MO adsorption equilibrium is reached much more rapidly than Cr(VI). We modeled the experimental data for Cr(VI) and MO adsorption on CS/XG/NCQDs at 25 °C by using three PFO, PSO, and intra-particle diffusion mathematical equations (Eqs. (5)–(7), SI) to ascertain the underlying mechanism of their adsorption. Table 2 gives details regarding the kinetic parameters of the XG/CS/NCQDs towards the two anionic pollutants listed there. Among the parameters presented are qe, rate constants, and correlation coefficients. Fig. 6 illustrates the fitted plots of all three kinetic models mentioned above. According to Table 2, both lines fitted by the PSO kinetic model for MO and Cr(VI) adsorption have correlation coefficients (R2: 0.9994 and 0.9989, respectively) that are higher than 0.99 and closer to 1 than their PFO counterparts. Regarding R2 values, a simulation using the PSO model produced plots that corresponded more closely to the MO and Cr(VI) adsorption kinetics data compared to the simulation using the PFO model. Additionally, the calculated values of adsorption quantity at equilibrium (qe,cal) for MO dye and Cr(VI) ions using the PSO model approximately matched more closely the experimentally determined adsorption capacities (qe,exp) than those calculated using the PFO model. MO and Cr(VI) adsorption is further demonstrated to be governed by a PSO kinetic model. As evidenced by a higher rate constant (k2) of MO adsorption by XG/CS/NCQDs nanocomposite than Cr(VI) adsorption, the nanocomposite adsorbs MO dye more rapidly than Cr(VI) ions. Through the use of an intra-particle diffusion model, the stages of adsorption were further explored. Plotting qt against t1/2 leads to two diffusion fitting stages and the two-step process is in progress (see Fig. 6). It is evident from this that two stages are involved in the adsorption process. A short-term (0–30 min for MO and 0–60 min for Cr) fit of the intra-particle diffusion model with the experimental data was found here. A high slope (Ki) was observed in the first stage (Table 2) due to exterior diffusion, which allowed two adsorbates from the aqueous medium to move rapidly to the XG/CS/NCQD surface. A second step involves the progressive diffusion of MO dye and Cr(VI) ions from the surface into the XG/CS/NCQDs pores and the equilibrium adsorption that follows. However, in the early stages of adsorption, the first stages don't appear to cross the origin, suggesting that intra-particle diffusion might not be the only mechanism driving adsorption, and there might be other mechanisms working concurrently as well.

With the increase in temperature, MO dye and Cr(VI) ions adsorb more slowly, and the duration of time for reaching the adsorption equilibrium was substantially prolonged, from 60 to 120 min after increasing the temperature ranging between 25 and 55 °C.

To determine whether medium temperature influences the adsorption of MO dye and Cr(VI) ions, temperature-dependent adsorption experiments were conducted. Temperature was found to influence MO and Cr(VI) adsorption on XG/CS/NCQDs biofilm. The change in this parameter should result in Cr(VI) and MO adsorbates being more mobile and soluble, and diffusing more readily in the solvent, thus exposing more adsorption sites. Fig. 8c depicts MO and Cr(VI) removal percentages on CS/XG/CQDs film over temperatures ranging from 25 to 55 °C at a concentration of 50 mg/L for MO and Cr(VI), and pH values of 4 and 2 for MO and Cr(VI), respectively. With a temperature increase of 25 °C to 55 °C, the calculated qe or R% values decreased. MO and Cr (VI) removal at 25 °C peaked at 97.5 % and 99.7 %, respectively, and diminished at elevated temperatures (to 85 % and 89 %). This implies that the adsorption process of both adsorbates took place exothermically. The interaction between MO and Cr(VI) with active sites on the adsorbent is most likely weak at high temperatures, and their interaction may also contribute to desorption. The interaction between MO molecules and Cr(VI) ions with solvent molecules becomes stronger as the temperature rises, leading to more dye solubility and Cr(VI) ion mobility, as well as decreasing adsorption performance. In light of these findings, we conducted subsequent adsorption experiments at a temperature of 25 °C. It is worth noting that in this case both pollutants' initial concentration was 50 mg/L and so their removal percentage and adsorption capacity were equal. We examined MO dye and Cr(VI) ions adsorption by XG/CS/NCQDs at temperatures ranging between 298 and 328 K and at optimal conditions (50 mg of adsorbent, 50 mg/L MO and Cr(VI), and pH 4 and 2 for MO and Cr(VI), respectively). Subsequently, the thermodynamic parameters like the changes in enthalpy (ΔHo kJ/mol) and entropy (ΔSo J/K mol) were estimated from the linear regression of lnKd vs 1/T using the slope and intercept of van't Hoff plots (Eq. (14), SI) to provide information about adsorption's nature and accompanying heat changes. The adsorption Gibbs free energy (ΔGo kJ/mol) was also determined by using Eqs. (11)–(13). Table S2 (SI) presents the extracted parameters' values. The XG/CS/NCQDs biofilm adsorb MO dye and Cr(VI) ions spontaneously with ΔG° negative values within the examined temperature limit, both of which are thermodynamically favorable (Table S2). The observed ΔGo values ranging between 0 and 40 (kJ/mol) indicate that physical adsorption prevails, where MO molecules and Cr(VI) ions mainly electrostatically interact with adsorption sites (Febrianto et al., 2009; Liu, Cui, et al., 2019). Low temperatures are advantageous for MO and Cr(VI) adsorption, as evidenced by the rise of ΔGo with temperature. It can be inferred from the negative values of ΔHo, which are −92.205 for MO and − 35.94 for Cr(VI), that they are likely to engage in exothermic interactions with surface binding sites associated with XG/CS/NCQDs. Moreover, MO and Cr(VI) adsorption is an exothermic process; as evidenced by reduced adsorption as solution temperatures rise (Fig. 8c). Furthermore, the XG/CS/NCQDs internal structure did not appear to be significantly altered by the adsorption of both adsorbates, as evidenced by a negligible entropy change. This means the two adsorbates are transitioning into a solid phase with less randomness from a solution phase, which is reflected in their slightly negative ΔSo values. Thus, enthalpy was the driving force behind the MO and Cr(VI) adsorption processes on the XG/CS/CQDs biofilm. In similar temperature conditions, the adsorption of MO showed a lower ΔHo value compared to Cr(VI) when comparing the adsorption thermodynamic parameters on XG/CS/NCQDs. According to the findings, MO can be absorbed by XG/CS/NCQDs at a higher energy than Cr(VI) (Niu et al., 2021). Based on the smaller ΔSo value of MO adsorption compared to Cr(VI) adsorption, there was a more ordered adsorption process for MO on XG/CS/NCQDs. Most likely, MO arranged in an ordered manner on the XG/CS/NCQD surface can be explained by MO's large and planar molecular structure resulting from van der Waal's forces (e.g., n-π interaction or π-π interaction) and hydrogen bonding.

Diverse co-existing anions commonly contaminate actual water samples, competing to some extent with anionic contaminants like Cr(VI) and MO for the adsorbent surface's available binding sites. To evaluate this effect, the adsorption performance of XG/CS/NCQDs biofilm was tested alongside selected co-existing ions like Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2− and CO3 2− with the objective of examining their respective interferences with Cr(VI) ions and MO dye separately. In separate experiments, both Cr(VI) and MO were mixed in the same concentration of interfering ions (10 to 30 mM). The results presented in Fig. 8 indicate that markedly diminished removal percentages for Cr(VI) and MO were observed under conditions where NO3 − or SO4 2− were present (except Cl− ions) at concentrations ranging from 10 to 30 mM. The removal percentage decreases for both adsorbates when co-anions are contained in the solution. This is likely caused by the existence of additional like-charged ions to bind to the still-unoccupied sites as a result of the electrostatic attraction, a process that could be significantly influenced by the existence of NO3 − or SO4 2− ions. In light of these findings, the electrostatic attraction appears to play a significant function in the adsorption mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recycling and reusing the prepared XG/CS/CQDs biofilm
ce:section.ce:para:
The ability of the spent XG/CS/CQDs adsorbent to be regenerated can be appealing since it indicates that it is stable and suggests that it is capable of being used repeatedly. To regenerate the MO dye and Cr(VI) ion-loaded XG/CS/NCQDs biofilm, desorption is advantageous for the release of both pollutants from it. After conducting two separate control experiments, the distilled water was found to be ineffective at desorbing MO dye or Cr(VI) ions, which indicates that both pollutants are retained on the nanocomposite hydrogel by a stably-adsorbed layer. Different basic NaOH and Na2CO3 solutions were utilized in an attempt to treat the MO and Cr(VI) loaded-XG/CS/NCQDs and regenerate the XG/CS/NCQDs. When using Na2CO3 solution of 0.1 M, the low desorption efficiency was observed which may be attributed to its weak basicity. To desorb both anionic contaminants from the spent nanocomposite hydrogel, NaOH solution was treated to regenerate the XG/CS/CQDs biofilm. A possible desorption process could be proposed as follows:
R −
NH 3
+
…
HCrO 4
−
…
+
H 3
N − R +
3OH −
→ 2R −
NH 2
+
CrO 4
2 −
+
3H 2
O

An alkaline solution (0.1 M NaOH) can adequately remove Cr(VI) ions and MO adsorbing XG/CS/NCQDs, possibly because the high pH reduces XG/CS/NCQDs adsorption capacity or removal efficiency. To assess the ability to recycle the nanocomposite hydrogel, the corresponding five desorption and adsorption cyclic was repeated consecutively. Based on Fig. 8, the hydrogel nanocomposite investigated in this study showed a decrease in the efficiency of removing MO dye and Cr(VI) ions that does not exceed 17 % and 15 %, respectively, in comparison to its initial efficiency at removing MO dye and Cr(VI). In light of the good regeneration properties of the XG/CS/NCQDs adsorbent capable of removing MO dye and Cr(VI) ions, it might be possible to use it for removing organic and inorganic pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of adsorption and fluorescent quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: A reduction in the adsorption efficiency of Cr(VI) and MO as the pH rises is shown in Fig. 5c, supporting that the positively charged XG/CS/NCQDs surface is electrostatically attached to the anionic species (Cr(VI) (existing as HCrO4 −) and MO). To study the Cr (VI) and MO adsorption of the synthesized XG/CS-based fluorescent self-assembling nanocomposite hydrogel incorporating NCQDs, FTIR analyses were performed to further elucidate the surface interactions that may contribute to adsorption. In Fig. 9 c and e, the FTIR spectra are depicted before and after MO and Cr(VI) have been adsorbed by the XG/CS/NCQDs. In comparison with pristine XG/CS/NCQDs, there were no noticeable absorption bands in the FT-IR spectra of XG/CS/NCQDs following the adsorption of Cr(VI) and MO, other than their characteristic absorption bands. Consequently, the structure of XG/CS/NCQDs remained chemically intact following adsorption. According to Fig. 9b, we can observe that the broad bands of NH and OH bonds in XG/CS/CQDs that correspond to stretching vibrations have partially moved to 3467 cm−1 and 3405 cm−1, respectively following Cr(VI) and MO adsorption. It is evident from this finding that via hydrogen bonding, the hydroxyl and amino groups of XG/CS/NCQDs served as adsorption sites for Cr(VI) ions and MO molecules. Following adsorption, glucopyranose's characteristic band near 1070 cm−1 diminished significantly, further supporting that amino- and hydroxyl group-containing structural units of CS and XG were actively engaged when absorbing. In the FTIR spectra of Cr(VI) and MO loaded-XG/CS/NCQDs biofilm, the ester groups have not been affected by either adsorbate. It was found that two samples had shoulders around 1726 cm−1. When XG/CS/NCQD FTIR spectra were compared before and after the adsorption of MO and Cr(VI), it was evident that the amide I (1633 cm−1) and amide II (1530 cm−1) bands moved down from 1606 cm−1 and 1523 cm−1 for MO, and 1621 cm−1 and 1522 cm−1 for Cr(VI), while other peaks shifted slightly. This suggests the involvement of amino groups in removing MO dye and Cr(VI) ions. Another obvious feature following the adsorption of Cr(VI) ions is the emergence of new absorption peaks at 937 cm−1 and 756 cm−1 associated with the asymmetric stretching vibrations of the CrO bond and the asymmetric vibrations of the Cr-O-Cr bridge bond in chromium species (Chen et al., 2022; Dong et al., 2020). The results above validate the XG/CS/NCQDs nanocomposite gel was capable of adsorbing Cr(VI) oxyanions.

Moreover, XRD, EDAX, and FE-SEM characterizations were performed on Cr(VI) and MO-loaded XG/CS/NCQDs (Fig. 9a and b). The obvious characteristic chromium and sulfur signals after the adsorption in the EDAX spectrum provided further evidence of XG/CS/NCQDs' remarkable ability to uptake Cr(VI) and MO. Further analysis of XG/CS/NCQDs biofilms loaded with Cr(VI) and MO was performed by FE-SEM. Before adsorption, there was a porous structure visible in Fig. 2a and b, which increased the swelling ratio, surface area, and improved both adsorption of adsorbates. The surface of the biofilm adsorbs Cr(VI) and MO, as evidenced by the aggregate layer or accumulation on the biofilms loaded with both adsorbate (Fig. 9). A dense microstructure was observed after adsorption as shown in Fig. 9b–d, suggesting that anionic species are strongly electrostatically/non-electrostatically interacting with functional groups (carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl). After applying the adsorbent to each of the adsorbate solutions, the pores of the XG/CS/NCQDs nanocomposite gel filled up, as can be seen in Figs. 2a and b and 9a and b. As shown by the images, the overall morphology after Cr(VI) and MO adsorption is denser than before, suggesting that both organic and inorganic species have been adsorbing on the hydrogel network and interacting strongly with the bio-nanocomposite film. Furthermore, Fig. 9d and f shows the XRD pattern comparison of oxyanion Cr2O7 2−, MO dye, XG/CS/NCQDs, and Cr(VI) and MO-loaded XG/CS/NCQDs. There were distinctly visible peaks in the XRD spectrum of MO dye and Cr2O7 2− between from 2θ = 15° to 32° and 10° to 35°, respectively, which are typical of MO dye and Cr2O7 2− oxyanion because they are crystalline (Badawy et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2017). The significant characteristic peaks of Cr(VI) exhibiting peaks between 15° and 32° did not exist in the XG/CS/NCQDs nanocomposite gel. We also found that the diffraction peaks corresponding to MO dye's crystalline structure reported in the literature in the range of 2θ from around 15° to 32° did not appear. These findings suggest that MO and Cr(VI) species became amorphous in the matrix of XG/CS/NCQDs. MO and Cr(VI) crystallization was limited by the strong interaction among CS, XG, and MO and CS, XG, and Cr(VI), respectively. Upon adsorption of MO, however, a greater degree of diffraction was observed, which is now evident from the XRD spectrum of hydrogel adsorbed with MO, indicating a slight increase in the crystallinity of XG/CS/NCQDs. In light of the results above, Cr(VI) and MO adsorption mechanisms on XG/CS/NCQDs nanocomposite with the 3D network were proposed (Scheme 2 ). It was primarily the electrostatic attraction that drove Cr(VI) adsorption. Cr(VI) oxyanion predominantly occurs in solution as negatively charged HCrO4 − at a weakly acidic pH of 2.0 (Rahmatpour & Alizadeh Hesarsorkh, 2024; Badawy et al., 2023; Samuel et al., 2018). Hence, the protonated -NH2 of CS and NCQDs (NH3 +) on the XG/CS/NCQDs framework provided the active sites for the favorable adsorption of negatively charged anion HCrO4 − through the strong electrostatic attraction (Zhang et al., 2015). The adsorption process on XG/CS/NCQs (R-NH2) is as follows:
R −
NH 2
+
H +
→ R −
NH 3
+
2R −
NH 3
+
+
HCrO 4
−
→ R −
NH 3
+
…
HCrO 4
−
…
+
H 3
N − R

There is also the possibility of HCrO4 − being adsorbed through the weak cation-π interaction between HCrO4 − and aromatic ring within the NCQDs framework (Abdelhameed et al., 2019; Niu et al., 2021).

Various interactions are likely to contribute to MO adsorption on XG/CS/NCQDs. It has been clarified in recent investigations that the degree of MO ionization reaches its peak at pKa = 3.46 and under weakly acidic conditions (pH = 4.0), MO is predominantly ionized in the solution and possesses the negatively charged -SO3 − terminal group (Karthikeyan et al., 2021; Samuel et al., 2018). Accordingly, in XG/CS/NCQDs, the protonated amino groups (NH3 +) of CS and NCQDs served as the active site for MO adsorption by electrostatically interacting strongly with negatively charged -SO3 − groups (Niu et al., 2021). MO dye uptake is primarily driven by electrostatic attraction, but non-electrostatic attractions are also considered to contribute to its adsorption on XG/CS/NCQDs. Among them are dipole-dipole hydrogen bonding between -OH and -N-H of (CS and XG) and the O and N atoms in the MO molecules (Abdulhameed, Jawad, & Mohammad, 2019) and n-π interactions between the lone pair electrons of O and N atoms in CS, XG and NCQDs and the aromatic ring in MO molecules. There are also π-π interactions between the aromatic rings in both NCQDs in XG/CS/NCQDs and MO molecule (Chen et al., 2019; Mohamed et al., 2020; Niu et al., 2021) and Yoshida hydrogen bonding between -OH of CS and aromatic ring of MO (Scheme 2) (Niu et al., 2021; Abdulhameed, Mohammad, & Jawad, 2019). In MO adsorbed XG/CS/NCQDs spectrum at ∼1370 cm−1, the CN band was observed to be intensified following the inclusion of the -SO3 − group of MO (Bahrudin et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2022). Furthermore, this fact implies that XG/CS/NCQDs might be capable of absorbing MO.

Taking everything into account, Scheme 2 depicts a possible mechanism to clarify Cr(VI) and MO anionic adsorption as well as to detect Cr(VI) using porous fluorescent gel XG/CS/NCQDs. As XG/CS/NCQDs adsorbent was immersed in a solution of two adsorbates, Cr(VI) ions/MO molecules are then adsorbed to the hydrogel surface through an electrostatic attraction between two anionic species and amino groups in the hydrogel. In the nanocomposite gel, two species were easily diffused from the outside to the inside via its porous 3D structure. The hydrogel's interior binding sites were then occupied by two anionic species through electrostatic interactions, non-electrostatic interactions, and complexation. Furthermore, NCQDs embedded and wrapped within the 3D porous structure of XG/CS/NCQDs functioned as fluorescent probes that detected Cr(VI). Cr(VI) ions quenched the fluorescence of XG/CS/NCQDs when they interacted with NCQDs containing the plentiful functional groups (-NH2, -COOH, -OH groups). In accordance with UV–vis absorption and FTIR spectra, these interactions were evident in hydrogels (Fig. 3a). In consequence, fluorescence quenching occurred when excited electrons from NCQDs' surface functional groups transferred non-radiative energy to unoccupied orbitals of Cr(VI) ions. By virtue of the unique structure of our newly prepared nanocomposite hydrogel, enhanced fluorescence signal stability was achieved and Cr(VI) detection sensitivity markedly improved.

To summarize, a novel self-assembled NCQDs embedded in a bio-nanocomposite comprised of a CS and XG gelling network capable of fluorescent sensing and adsorbing Cr(VI) and MO has been developed through a simple, cost-effective, and eco-friendly approach involving aqueous physical gelation at low temperature. Neither synthetic polymers nor potentially hazardous small-molecule cross-linkers were employed during the production process, and the synthesized nanocomposite hydrogel was composed exclusively of XG and CS that occur naturally in the environment. A nanocomposite hydrogel of XG/CS/NCQDs was studied in terms of its morphology, synthesis mechanism, chemical structure, swelling properties, and optical properties. The nanocomposite gel featured an integrated 3D network structure that self-assembled, incorporating NCQDs containing carboxylic acid, hydroxyl, and amino groups that served as crosslinking sites thanks to strong hydrogen bonds between NCQDs and the matrix of the XG and CS, both of which contain OH groups. In response to UV excitation, XG/CS/NCQDs nanocomposite gel emits stable blue fluorescence. The swelling percentage index increased markedly when NCQD nanoparticles were incorporated into the polymer network, so that without NCQDs, the maximum swelling percentage was 9800 % whereas with NCQDs, it was 32,800 %. A SEM analysis and swelling results confirm the good adsorptive ability of synthesized nanocomposites in adsorbing the anionic pollutants of MO and Cr(VI) from aqueous solution. As for Cr(VI) and MO, their adsorption isotherms followed Langmuir's model, and their adsorption kinetics matched the pseudo-second-order, and their respective adsorption capacities were 291 and 456 mg/g, respectively. The XG/CS/NCQDs fluorescent hydrogel showed remarkable selectivity and sensitivity towards Cr(VI) ions, exhibiting a linear response range (F/F0 vs. Cr(VI) concentration) of 1–100 μM, a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9929 and a detection limit of 0.015 mg/L. Based on FE-SEM, TEM, and FTIR analyses, adding NCQDs in the XG/CS matrix contributed to the development of a porous structure that facilitates mass transfer of both adsorbates and provides increased binding sites as well as enhanced sensitivity and visibility to Cr(VI), thereby significantly improving anionic pollutants adsorption and detection of Cr(VI). Overall, the results indicate that XG/CS/NCQDs nanocomposite hydrogel has an outstanding potential to be used as a fluorescent detector and for adsorbing dissolved cationic and anionic organic pollutants in wastewater.

Ali Rahmatpour: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Amir Hossein Alizadeh Hesarsorkh: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation.
